skill.name: ensembl-database
skill.description: Query Ensembl REST APIs for gene lookup, sequence retrieval, variant annotation, assembly mapping, and comparative genomics research.
skill.summary: Access Ensembl genomic data through REST examples, endpoint references, and a reusable Python query helper.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution and blocker patterns, but review found they are false positives from Markdown backticks and genomics terms. The skill includes a Python script that makes expected HTTPS requests to Ensembl REST APIs, with no evidence of malicious endpoints, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or shell execution.

low_findings.title: Expected External Ensembl API Access
low_findings.description: The helper script performs HTTPS GET and POST requests to Ensembl REST servers. This is core functionality, but users should expect outbound network traffic and public query submission.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The requests are direct calls to documented Ensembl domains and match the skill purpose. I found no evidence of credential handling or unrelated external endpoints.

low_findings.title: No Request Timeout In HTTP Calls
low_findings.description: The Python helper uses requests without explicit timeouts. This can cause long waits or stalled workflows if the remote API is slow or unreachable.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are visible and no timeout argument is present on the cited requests calls. This is a reliability and availability concern, not malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static High-Risk Patterns Dismissed As False Positives
low_findings.description: Markdown code fences were misclassified as shell backticks, and GA4GH beacon plus database terminology triggered malware and weak-crypto signatures. Review found no shell execution or malicious intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation, normal Python examples, or argparse text. Targeted review found no subprocess, eval, os.system, prompt injection, or suspicious beaconing.

content.value_statement: Genomic research often needs reliable access to Ensembl genes, variants, sequences, and assemblies. This skill provides focused guidance, endpoint references, and a reusable query script.
actual_capabilities: Looks up genes by symbol or Ensembl identifier.
Retrieves DNA, transcript, and protein sequences.
Queries variant records and VEP consequence predictions.
Finds orthologs, paralogs, and gene tree information.
Maps coordinates between genome assemblies such as GRCh37 and GRCh38.
Provides a Python helper with retries and rate-limit handling.
limitations: Requires network access to Ensembl REST services.
Does not provide clinical interpretation or medical advice.
Results depend on current Ensembl releases and API availability.
The helper script does not add request timeouts by default.

use_cases.title: Annotate Research Genes
use_cases.description: Retrieve coordinates, transcripts, proteins, and external references for genes used in a study.

use_cases.title: Review Variant Consequences
use_cases.description: Use Ensembl variant and VEP endpoints to summarize predicted effects and population data.

use_cases.title: Compare Genes Across Species
use_cases.description: Find orthologs, paralogs, and gene tree data for comparative genomics workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Look Up A Gene
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Ensembl database skill to look up BRCA2 in human. Summarize coordinates, transcripts, and external references.

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve A Sequence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Ensembl to retrieve the genomic sequence for ENSG00000139618. Include format options and note rate-limit handling.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze A Variant
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Ensembl variation and VEP endpoints to review rs699 in human. Summarize predicted consequences and population fields.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Comparative Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an Ensembl workflow for BRCA2 ortholog comparison across human, mouse, and zebrafish. Include endpoint order and caching advice.

output_examples.output: A concise gene summary with Ensembl ID, chromosome location, transcript overview, and links to external database references.

output_examples.input: Find mouse orthologs for a human gene.
output_examples.output: A comparative table summary listing ortholog identifiers, species, homology type, and confidence notes.
A variant report with genomic location, observed alleles, population frequency fields, and VEP consequence categories.

best_practices: Use batch endpoints when querying many identifiers.
Cache stable Ensembl responses for repeat workflows.
Respect rate limits and Retry-After headers.
anti_patterns: Do not send private clinical data without review.
Do not treat VEP output as medical diagnosis.
Do not ignore assembly version differences.

faq.question: What data source does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses the public Ensembl REST API and documented Ensembl endpoints.

faq.question: Can it query older human assemblies?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents the GRCh37 REST server and assembly mapping workflows.

faq.question: Does it require an API key?
faq.answer: No API key is shown. Anonymous Ensembl requests are rate limited.

faq.question: Can it predict variant effects?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Ensembl VEP endpoints for HGVS, variant ID, and region inputs.

faq.question: Is it suitable for clinical decisions?
faq.answer: No. It supports research workflows and does not provide medical interpretation.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
